title: No Warning Before the Dam Burst
subtitle: Twenty-six of thirty-nine Guangxi deaths came from the Liulan Reservoir. Residents say they received no alert. China's state-aligned press covered the jasmine fields.
body_md:
Typhoon Maysak struck Guangxi, China, killing thirty-nine people — twenty-six of them in a single event: the burst of the Liulan Reservoir dam. [Hong Kong Free Press](https://hongkongfp.com/2026/07/09/cleanup-operations-underway-as-death-toll-from-china-floods-hits-39/) carried the only full body extraction from the disaster zone. Its coverage included on-the-ground testimony: "We never received any warning. If we had received a warning, our losses would have been much less." A resident described flooding unprecedented in living memory. No other outlet in this corpus of twenty-four outlets across thirteen countries published that account.

China's state-aligned [Sixth Tone](https://www.sixthtone.com/news/1018748/Four Dead, Eight Missing as Typhoon Maysak Batters Guangxi) covered the disaster differently: jasmine fields inundated during peak tea-harvest season, framing the catastrophe through cultural-economic loss rather than the dam-failure accountability question. Both HKFP and Sixth Tone are China-adjacent outlets. The divergence in what each chose to report is the most analytically significant editorial gap in this corpus.

**The shared frame**

[Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/7/9/flooding-from-tropical-storm-maysak-kills-39-in-southern-china?traffic_source=rss) led with the evacuation: "About 130,000 people have been evacuated from the Guangxi region while thousands of rescuers have been deployed." [BBC World](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cx2wkzww20ro?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss) reported "rescuers pull bodies from the mud after heavy monsoon rains batter the south-eastern coast." Most outlets across the corpus combined the Maysak death count with anticipatory reporting on Super Typhoon Bavi, which meteorologists were describing as potentially the strongest typhoon since 2024, bearing down on Taiwan and Fujian with landfall expected July 11. The two stories merged in coverage into a compound extreme-weather event.

**The deviations**

[Malay Mail](https://www.malaymail.com/news/world/2026/07/07/xi-calls-for-all-out-rescue-as-storms-tornadoes-and-landslide-kill-15-injure-hundreds-and-displace-thousands-in-china/226735) and [The Guardian](https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jul/07/china-severe-storms-death-toll-xi-jinping-rescue-efforts-landslides-tornadoes) both covered Xi Jinping's "all out" rescue directive — a political frame that situated state response as a visible governance act. [South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/hong-kong/transport/article/3359975/super-typhoon-bavi-dozens-hong-kong-flights-cancelled-storm-nears-taiwan?utm_source=rss_feed) focused on flight cancellations and the commercial disruption from Bavi's track rather than Guangxi deaths.

[VnExpress](https://e.vnexpress.net/news/news/environment/around-900-snakes-escape-flooded-farm-after-typhoon-maysak-hits-southern-china-5094878.html) alone reported that approximately 900 snakes escaped a flooded snake farm in Hengzhou, Guangxi, with villagers joining rescue workers in the search. No other outlet in the corpus carried this. It is both an ecological-agricultural angle and a piece of local texture that Vietnamese regional coverage apparently prioritised where wire services and Western broadsheets did not.

The Rohingya camp disaster in Cox's Bazar — landslides killing at least thirteen people including children struck while attending class — was tracked primarily by Indian and UK outlets. [NDTV](https://www.ndtv.com/world-news/bangladesh-rohingya-camp-landslide-monsoon-landslide-kills-five-children-at-rohingya-refugee-camp-in-bangladesh-11746822#publisher=newsstand) reported eight early deaths; [The Hindu](https://www.thehindu.com/news/international/many-children-die-in-monsoon-landslide-at-a-rohingya-camp-in-bangladesh/article71198792.ece) noted "more than 1 million refugees live in the camp" and that the landslide hit as children were attending classes. Southeast Asian press concentrated on the regional typhoon track and gave the Bangladesh deaths limited coverage.

[Sixth Tone](https://www.sixthtone.com/news/1018693/Floods Hit Northwest and Southern China After Record Rainfall) was also the only outlet to invoke El Niño as a causal driver, quoting experts warning that its return may partly explain escalating extreme weather events in China.

**The silence**

[Philstar](https://www.philstar.com/weather/2026/07/09/2540971/typhoon-inday-weakens-slightly-signal-no-2-over-batanes) covered the typhoon exclusively under the Philippine domestic name "Typhoon Inday" with Signal No. 2 alerts over Batanes — no coverage of the thirty-nine deaths in Guangxi, no mention of the Bangladesh Rohingya landslides. Japan Times covered only flight and ferry cancellations from Bavi's track toward remote Japanese islands. Both silences reflect the same editorial logic: a regional weather event is covered as a domestic threat, not as a cross-border humanitarian story.

[Antara English](https://en.antaranews.com/news/421952/cyclone-bavi-threatens-eastern-indonesia-with-strong-winds-and-waves) was the only outlet to report Indonesia's exposure to Bavi — an impact entirely absent from all other corpus coverage.

**Stakes**

Thirty-nine deaths, 130,000 displaced, and Super Typhoon Bavi still approaching as of July 9. The dam failure warning gap reported by HKFP is the kind of accountability question that matters more in retrospect than at the moment of initial coverage — which is precisely why it tends to get lost in multi-disaster compound events of this scale. The corpus documented the deaths. Only one outlet asked what residents were or were not told before the reservoir gave way.
